
title: "Carmine Gate Problem v23.41"

  description: "At 21:05, the Carmine Gate artifact vanished from a locked display in the southwestern gallery. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) each had valid logs near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all their statements; the other three speak only truths. There is only one real culprit. Each suspect offers four statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'I left at 21:00, so I cannot be the thief.'
      (2) 'If 2 is lying, then 3 must be the one who took it.'
      (3) '4 was nowhere near the display at 21:05—this I am sure.'
      (4) '2 left the gallery at 20:55, before I did.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 stayed until 21:10, contradicting 1’s 21:00 claim.'
      (2) 'I never touched the Carmine Gate after 20:50, so I am innocent.'
      (3) 'If 3 is telling the truth, then 4’s statements are all false.'
      (4) 'Either 1 is lying, or 3 is guilty—but not both.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not approach that display after 20:45.'
      (2) '2 is not the culprit if 4 is speaking truthfully.'
      (3) '1’s claim that 4 was absent at 21:05 is false—4 might have been there.'
      (4) 'If 1 is lying, 2 must be telling complete truth.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was definitely near the display exactly at 21:05, contrary to 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'I am not guilty; my logs prove I was in a different section.'
      (3) 'If 2 is truthful, then 1’s timeline is incorrect.'
      (4) 'Either 3 is lying, or 2 is guilty—but not both.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Carmine Gate? Please return only one digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Extended Formal Logic:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all four statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  We know exactly one suspect L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, others T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 1 did not leave 21:00, might be culprit, 'If 2 lying => 3 culprit' is not correct => no forced scenario, '4 was absent 21:05' is false => 4 might be present, '2 left 20:55' is false => 2 stayed longer. Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => 2(1) '1 stayed until 21:10,' 3(3) '1 is lying re 4’s presence at 21:05' all consistent, but also blame logic leads to contradiction with 4(1). Typically fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
    - (1) '1 stayed until 21:10' false => 1’s 21:00 claim stands
    - (2) '2 never touched after 20:50 => innocent' false => 2 might be culprit
    - (3) 'If 3 is truth => 4 is all false' not correct => no forced scenario
    - (4) 'Either 1 is lying or 3 is guilty but not both' is false => so possibly both or neither. Then 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(1) 'Left 21:00 => not thief' => G(1)=false. 3(2) '2 not culprit if 4 is truth' is it? Checking 4(2) 'I not guilty => logs prove different section' => G(4)=false => so suspect 3’s statement 3(1) might also be consistent => we do find 2 is culprit, no contradiction emerges.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
    - (1) false => 3 approached after 20:45 => potential guilt
    - (2) false => '2 not culprit if 4 truthful' is not correct => 2 can be culprit or 4 untruthful
    - (3) false => '1’s claim re 4 absent 21:05 is false' is not correct => so maybe 4 indeed was absent
    - (4) false => 'If 1 is lying => 2 is all truth' is also not correct => big puzzle. Then 1,2,4 are truthful => times lead to contradiction with 4(1) or 2(4). Typically fails.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3),(4) false => 
    - (1) false => '3 near display 21:05' not true => 3 might be honest about not approaching
    - (2) false => 'I not guilty => logs prove different section' is false => 4 can be culprit
    - (3) false => 'If 2 truth => 1 timeline is incorrect' no forced scenario
    - (4) false => 'Either 3 lying or 2 guilty but not both' is untrue => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,2,3 are truthful => 1(1) 'Left 21:00 => not thief' => G(1)=false, 2(2) 'I not near after 20:50 => not guilty' => G(2)=false, 3(2) '2 not culprit if 4 truth' but 4 is not truthful => no direct clash. Possibly G(4). Actually no contradiction arises. This scenario can hold with G(4)=true.

  Checking carefully between L(2) => G(2) or L(4) => G(4). Usually puzzle yields a single unique solution. Evaluate who 3(2) exonerates if 4 is truthful. But 4 is not truthful if L(4). So 3(2) says '4 not guilty => saw them different section' but 3 is truthful if L(4)? Wait, that implies G(4) or G(2)? We see no contradiction. So L(4) => G(4) can also be consistent. We must see which is actually contradictory. If 1 is truthful => 1(3) '4 absent 21:05' vs 4(1) false => '3 near 21:05' => 3 not near => G(3)=false => 4 is culprit. That scenario works with no contradiction. So G(4).  

  final: '4'
"

  final: "4"
